Question: Which of the following passages is the original verbatim passage? The other passages are paraphrased versions of it.

Options:
A. The dignity of Viscount Knollys (), originating from Caversham in the County of Oxford, represents a peerage within the Peerage of the United Kingdom. This distinction was established in 1911 specifically for Francis Knollys, the 1st Baron Knollys, a distinguished court official who held the position of Private Secretary to the Sovereign from 1901 until 1913. He had previously been granted the title of Baron Knollys, also associated with Caversham in the County of Oxford, on July 21, 1902. His offspring, the second Viscount, undertook the responsibilities of Governor of Bermuda. As of the year 2023, these hereditary titles are presently held by the grandson of the aforementioned second Viscount, who is the fourth Viscount and assumed the titles in 2023. The third Viscountess Knollys (deceased in 2022) was the sister of Baron Farnham; she performed duties as the Vice Lord-Lieutenant of Norfolk. The individuals holding the title of Viscount Knollys are part of the notable Knollys family (pronounced "Nohlz") and trace their lineage through the direct male line from
B. Viscount Knollys (), of Caversham in the County of Oxford, is a title in the Peerage of the United Kingdom. It was created in 1911 for the court official Francis Knollys, 1st Baron Knollys, Private Secretary to the Sovereign from 1901 to 1913. He had been previously created Baron Knollys, of Caversham in the County of Oxford, on 21 July 1902. His son, the second Viscount, served as Governor of Bermuda. As of 2023 the titles are held by the latter's grandson, the fourth Viscount, who succeeded in 2023. The third Viscountess Knollys (d. 2022) was a sister of Baron Farnham: she served as Vice Lord-Lieutenant of Norfolk.The Viscounts Knollys are members of the prominent Knollys family (pronounced "Nohlz") and are descended in the senior male line from
C. In the Peerage of the United Kingdom, the title of Viscount Knollys of Caversham in the County of Oxford was bestowed upon Francis Knollys, 1st Baron Knollys, Private Secretary to the Sovereign from 1901 to 1913, who was created as Baron Knollys of Caversham in the County of Oxford in 1911.
